course_info.title: International Studies Internship
course_info.meeting_schedule: Internship placement (minimum 120 hours) plus bi-weekly seminars
prerequisites: IS411 - Research Methods in International Studies

class_schedule.topic: Internship Orientation: Expectations, Learning Objectives, and Professional Development
learning_objectives: Understand internship requirements, expectations, and assessment criteria
Set individual learning objectives aligned with career goals
Develop a professional development plan for the internship period
readings: Selected readings on professional skills in international affairs

lecture_notes.introduction: The internship bridges academic knowledge and professional practice. This orientation prepares you to maximize your learning and contribution in your placement.
key_concepts: Internship requirements: 120+ hours, supervisor evaluation, reflective journal, analytical paper
Setting SMART learning objectives for the placement
Professional conduct: communication, confidentiality, workplace culture
Networking and informational interviews
Connecting academic knowledge to professional practice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: drafting individual learning objectives and professional development plans

review.value: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound goals that guide your internship experience and provide criteria for self-assessment
The discipline of systematically reflecting on professional experiences to extract lessons, connect theory to practice, and develop professional judgment

class_schedule.topic: The International Affairs Job Market: Careers and Pathways
learning_objectives: Map career pathways in international affairs: government, IO, NGO, private sector, academia
Analyze how international studies skills translate to professional competencies
Develop a personal career strategy informed by internship experience
readings: Careers in International Affairs (SAIS guide excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Your internship is a stepping stone to a career. Understanding the international affairs job market helps you position yourself strategically.
key_concepts: Career sectors: foreign service, intelligence, international organizations, NGOs, think tanks, consulting, journalism, private sector
Core competencies: analysis, writing, languages, cultural competence, research, policy
The informational interview: building networks and gathering intelligence
Graduate school: MA, JD, MPP, and PhD pathways
Building a professional portfolio
lecture_notes.demonstration: Career mapping exercise: identifying target roles and the competencies needed for each

review.value: Analytical writing, policy analysis, research skills, cultural competence, foreign languages, quantitative literacy, and communication -- skills that transfer across government, IO, NGO, and private sector roles
A conversation with a professional in your target field to learn about their career path, daily work, and advice -- the most effective networking and career exploration tool

learning_objectives: Share initial internship experiences and observations
Identify connections between academic concepts and workplace realities
Discuss challenges and strategies for maximizing internship value
readings: Selected readings relevant to student placement sectors

lecture_notes.introduction: The bi-weekly seminar is where you process your internship experience through an analytical lens, sharing insights and learning from each other's placements.
key_concepts: Theory-practice connections: where do academic concepts appear in your work?
Organizational analysis: understanding your placement organization's mission, structure, and constraints
Professional communication: writing for different audiences
Navigating workplace dynamics: hierarchy, culture, politics
Building relationships and adding value beyond assigned tasks
lecture_notes.demonstration: Roundtable: each student shares one insight from their placement connecting theory to practice

review.value: The frequent disconnect between how international affairs is studied in the classroom and how it is practiced in organizations -- bridging this gap is a key learning objective
Understanding your placement organization as a case study: its mandate, institutional culture, resource constraints, stakeholder relationships, and how decisions are actually made

class_schedule.topic: Seminar: Professional Skills in International Affairs
learning_objectives: Develop professional writing skills for different formats: memos, briefs, reports
Discuss ethical dilemmas encountered in professional settings
readings: Ethics case studies in international affairs

lecture_notes.introduction: Professional skills are what transform knowledge into impact. Writing, briefing, and navigating ethical dilemmas are daily requirements in international affairs careers.
key_concepts: Professional writing formats: policy memo, briefing note, situation report, talking points
Briefing skills: concise, structured, audience-appropriate communication
Ethical dilemmas: confidentiality, conflicting loyalties, moral ambiguity
Time management and prioritization in high-pressure environments
Self-advocacy: asking for meaningful work and feedback
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing workshop: drafting a one-page policy memo on a topic from your internship

review.value: A concise document (1-2 pages) summarizing a policy issue and recommending action for a decision-maker, requiring clear thinking, brevity, and an action-oriented structure

The skill of recognizing and thoughtfully responding to ethical dilemmas in professional settings: when to speak up, when to defer, and how to maintain integrity while respecting institutional constraints

class_schedule.topic: Seminar: Mid-Internship Review and Analytical Paper Planning
learning_objectives: Plan the analytical paper connecting internship experience to academic knowledge
Discuss organizational effectiveness and policy implementation
readings: Selected academic literature relevant to student analytical paper topics

lecture_notes.introduction: At the midpoint, we assess progress, recalibrate goals, and begin planning the analytical paper that synthesizes your academic and professional learning.
key_concepts: Mid-point assessment: what have you learned, what remains?
Analytical paper: not a diary but an analytical connection between experience and scholarship
Research question for the analytical paper: derived from internship observation
Policy implementation: the gap between policy design and real-world execution
Professional growth: what skills have you developed?
lecture_notes.demonstration: Workshop: developing research questions and outlines for analytical papers

review.value: A 3000-word paper that analyzes an issue encountered during the internship through the lens of international studies scholarship -- not a description of what you did but an analysis of what you observed and learned

The common observation that policies designed in headquarters look different when implemented in the field, due to resource constraints, local politics, bureaucratic dynamics, and unintended consequences

class_schedule.topic: Seminar: Final Presentations and Professional Reflection
learning_objectives: Present analytical paper findings to seminar
Reflect on professional growth and career implications
Articulate the value of the internship for future career development

lecture_notes.introduction: The final seminar is where you share what you have learned, reflect on your professional growth, and prepare to leverage your internship experience going forward.
key_concepts: Presenting professional experiences analytically
Lessons learned: skills developed, knowledge gained, networks built
Career implications: how the internship shapes your next steps
Letter of recommendation strategy
Building on the internship: turning a placement into a career opportunity
lecture_notes.demonstration: Final presentations: each student presents their analytical paper findings and professional reflections

review.value: The practice of honestly assessing your professional growth: what you did well, what you struggled with, what you would do differently, and how the experience shapes your career direction
Converting your internship into career capital through strong supervisor relationships, reference letters, professional network connections, portfolio evidence, and a refined career strategy

major_assignments.title: Internship Analytical Paper and Professional Portfolio
major_assignments.description: Produce a 3000-word analytical paper connecting an issue encountered during the internship to international studies scholarship, accompanied by a professional portfolio documenting your internship experience and professional development.
objectives: Analyze a professional experience through an academic lens
Demonstrate the ability to connect theory to practice
Document professional growth and skill development
deliverables: 3000-word analytical paper connecting internship to scholarship
Professional portfolio: updated resume, work samples, supervisor evaluation

criteria: Clear analytical question derived from internship experience
Thoughtful analysis connecting theory to observed practice
Honest reflection on challenges and growth
Evidence of deepening analytical thinking over time
Connections between daily work and broader course themes
Portfolio is presentable to potential employers
Lessons learned are insightful and actionable
Rigorous analytical connection between internship and scholarship
Professional portfolio ready for career use

analytical_paper.description: Analytical paper connecting internship to international studies scholarship

reflective_journal.description: Weekly reflective journal entries documenting learning and growth

professional_portfolio.description: Resume, work samples, and supervisor evaluation

seminar_participation.description: Active engagement in bi-weekly seminars and peer feedback

supervisor_evaluation.description: Workplace supervisor assessment of professional performance

course_policies.attendance: Attendance at all bi-weekly seminars is mandatory. Missing a seminar without prior approval results in a 5% grade deduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Journal entries must be submitted weekly. Late analytical papers penalized 5% per day.
course_policies.collaboration: Seminar discussions are collaborative. The analytical paper must be individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All written work must be original. Confidential information from the internship must be anonymized or excluded per organizational policy.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to academic databases for the analytical paper. Professional tools as required by the placement.
